q: The value of the integral $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty \dfrac{\log_e(x)}{x^2 + 4}\,dx$ is:
question: The value of the integral $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty \dfrac{\log_e(x)}{x^2 + 4}\,dx$ is:
solution:
Let $I = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty \dfrac{\log_e(x)}{x^2 + 4}\,dx$

Substitute $x = 2 \tan \theta$, which gives $dx = 2 \sec^2 \theta \,d\theta$.

The limits of integration change from $x = 0 \to \theta = 0$ to $x \to \infty \to \theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

$I = \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \dfrac{\log_e(2 \tan \theta)}{4 \tan^2 \theta + 4} (2 \sec^2 \theta) \,d\theta$

$I = \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \dfrac{\log_e(2 \tan \theta)}{4 \sec^2 \theta} (2 \sec^2 \theta) \,d\theta$

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \log_e(2 \tan \theta) \,d\theta$

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} (\log_e 2 + \log_e(\tan \theta)) \,d\theta$

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \log_e 2 \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} 1 \,d\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \log_e(\tan \theta) \,d\theta$

Let $I_1 = \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \log_e(\tan \theta) \,d\theta$

Using the property $\displaystyle\int_0^a f(x)\,dx = \displaystyle\int_0^a f(a-x)\,dx$, we get:

$I_1 = \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \log_e\left(\tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right)\right) \,d\theta = \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \log_e(\cot \theta) \,d\theta$

$I_1 = \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \log_e\left(\dfrac{1}{\tan \theta}\right) \,d\theta = -\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \log_e(\tan \theta) \,d\theta = -I_1$

$2I_1 = 0 \Rightarrow I_1 = 0$

Substituting $I_1 = 0$ back into the equation for $I$:

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \log_e 2 \left[\theta\right]_0^{\pi/2} + 0$

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \log_e 2 \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) = \dfrac{\pi \log_e 2}{4}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\pi\log_e(2)}{4}$

question:

(where $[x]$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x) is
solution:
Let $f(x)=\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{\left[\frac{x}{\pi}\right]+\frac{1}{2}}$.

$\begin{array}{l}

\text { So, } f(-x)=\frac{\sin ^{2}(-x)}{\left[\frac{-x}{\pi}\right]+\frac{1}{2}} \quad \because[-x]=-1-[x] \\

\Rightarrow f(-x)=\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{-1-\left[\frac{x}{\pi}\right]+\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{-\frac{1}{2}-\left[\frac{x}{\pi}\right]}=-f(x)

\end{array}$

$\Rightarrow f(x)$ is odd function

Hence, $\int_{-2}^{2} f(x) d x=0$

solution:
Let $I=24 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\sin \left|4 x-\frac{\pi}{2}\right|+[2 \sin x]\right) d x$   ...(i)
Now $\left|4 x-\frac{\pi}{12}\right|= \begin{cases}-4 x+\frac{\pi}{12} & ; x < \frac{\pi}{48} \\ 4 x-\frac{\pi}{12} & ; \quad x \geq \frac{\pi}{48}\end{cases}$
$\therefore$ from $(\mathrm{i})$
$\begin{aligned} & I=24 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{48}}-\sin \left(4 x-\frac{\pi}{12}\right) d x+\int_{\frac{\pi}{48}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \sin \left(4 x-\frac{\pi}{12}\right) \\ & +\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}}[2 \sin x] d x+\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}[2 \sin x] d x \\ & I=24\left[\frac{\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{12}\right)}{4}-\frac{\left(-\cos \frac{\pi}{12}-1\right)}{4}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & I=24\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & I=2 \pi+12=2 \pi+\alpha \text { (from above) } \\ & \therefore \quad \alpha=12\end{aligned}$

q: $6 \int_{0}^{\pi}|(\sin 3 x+\sin 2 x+\sin x)| d x$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: $6 \int_{0}^{\pi}|(\sin 3 x+\sin 2 x+\sin x)| d x$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Using $\sin 3x + \sin x = 2\sin 2x \cos x$:
$\sin 3x + \sin 2x + \sin x = \sin 2x(2\cos x + 1) = 2\sin x \cos x(2\cos x + 1)$
Zeros in $[0, \pi]$: $x = 0, \pi/2, 2\pi/3, \pi$
Expression is positive on $(0, \pi/2)$, negative on $(\pi/2, 2\pi/3)$, positive on $(2\pi/3, \pi)$
Let $F(x) = -\frac{\cos 3x}{3} - \frac{\cos 2x}{2} - \cos x$
$F(0) = -\frac{11}{6}$, $F(\pi/2) = \frac{1}{2}$, $F(2\pi/3) = \frac{5}{12}$, $F(\pi) = \frac{5}{6}$
$\int_0^{\pi/2} = \frac{7}{3}$, $\left|\int_{\pi/2}^{2\pi/3}\right| = \frac{1}{12}$, $\int_{2\pi/3}^{\pi} = \frac{5}{12}$
Total $= \frac{7}{3} + \frac{1}{12} + \frac{5}{12} = \frac{17}{6}$
$6 \times \frac{17}{6} = 17$

q: Let $\int_0^x \sqrt{1-\left(y^{\prime}(t)\right)^2} d t=\int_0^x y(t) d t, 0 \leq x \leq 3, y \geq 0, y(0)=0$. Then at $x=2, y^{\prime \prime}+y+1$ is equal to
question: Let $\int_0^x \sqrt{1-\left(y^{\prime}(t)\right)^2} d t=\int_0^x y(t) d t, 0 \leq x \leq 3, y \geq 0, y(0)=0$. Then at $x=2, y^{\prime \prime}+y+1$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{1-\left(y^{\prime}(x)\right)^2}=y(x) \\ & 1-\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=y^2 \\ & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=1-y^2\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{\sqrt{1-y^2}}=d x \text { OR } \frac{d y}{\sqrt{1-y^2}}=-d x \\ & \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} y=x+c, \sin ^{-1} y=-x+c \\ & x=0, y=0 \Rightarrow c=0 \\ & \sin ^{-1} y=x, \text { as } y \geq 0 \\ & \sin x=y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos x \\ & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=-\sin x \\ & \Rightarrow-\sin x+\sin x+1=1\end{aligned}$

q: If for $\mathrm{n} \geq 1, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}=\int_1^{\mathrm{e}}\left(\log \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}\right) \mathrm{dx}$, then $\mathrm{P}_{10}-90 \mathrm{P}_8$ is equal to:
question: If for $\mathrm{n} \geq 1, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}=\int_1^{\mathrm{e}}\left(\log \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}\right) \mathrm{dx}$, then $\mathrm{P}_{10}-90 \mathrm{P}_8$ is equal to:
solution:
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}=\int_1^e(\log x)^n d x$ put $\log x=t$ then $x=e^t$ and $d x=e^t d t$
Also, when $x=1$, then $t=\log 1=0$ and when $x=e$, then $t=\log _e e=1$
$$
\begin{gathered}
\therefore \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}=\int_0^1 t^n \cdot e^t d t \\
\therefore \mathrm{P}_{10}=\int_0^1 t^{10} e^t d t \text { and } \mathrm{P}_8=\int_0^1 t^8 e^t d t \\
\text { Now, } \mathrm{P}_{10}-90 \mathrm{P}_8=\int_0^1 t^{10} e^t d t-90 \int_0^1 t^8 e^t d t \\
\mathrm{P}_{10}-90 \mathrm{P}_8 \\
\mathrm{P}_{10}-90 \mathrm{P}_8 \\
=\left[t^{10} e^t\right]_0^1-10 \int_0^1 t^9 e^t d t-90 \int_0^1 t^8 e^t d t \\
\mathrm{P}_{10}-90 \mathrm{P}_8= \\
e-10\left[t^9 \int_0^1 e^t d t-\int_0^1 \frac{d}{d t}\left(t^9\right) \int e^t d t\right]-90 \int_0^1 t^8 e^t d t
\end{gathered}
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{P}_{10}-90 \mathrm{P}_8=e-10\left[e-9 \int_0^1 t^8 e^t d t\right]-90 \int_0^1 t^8 e^t d t \\
\mathrm{P}_{10}-90 \mathrm{P}_8=e-10 e+90 \int t^8 e^t d t-90 \int_0^1 t^8 e^t d t \\
\therefore P_{10}-90 P_8=-9 e
\end{gathered}
$$

question: If <math><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> is a differentiable function and <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></munder><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></mrow></msubsup><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mfrac></math> is:
solution:
The given limit can be written as

l=limx→2∫6fx2tdtx-2  

Applying L' Hospital’s rule i.e. if l=limx→agxhx and limx→agx→0 & limx→ahx→0, then l=limx→ag'xh'x

l=limx→2ddx∫6fx2tdt1

Now, applying Newton's Leibnitz rule i.e. ddx∫abgxdx=gb·b'-ga·a', we get

⇒l=limx→22fx·f'x-0

⇒l=2f2⋅f'2

Put the given value, to get

⇒l=2×6×f'2=12f'2.

 k=∫0π2cosyfydy

fx=x+sinxπ2-1+k2sinx×1

fx=x+(π-2)sinx2+ksinx2   ...iii

from (i) and (iii)

k=π-2

Hence,  f(x)=x+(π-2)sinx
